summary: Gruber dissects Jeff LaMarche's defense of Apple's rejection of Rogue Amoeba's Airfoil Speakers Touch app update, systematically dismantling each of LaMarche's four points. He argues that the app never contained Apple-owned images directly — it displayed icons sent over the network from a Mac using public APIs. After closely reading the iPhone SDK Agreement, Gruber finds no clause that Airfoil Speakers Touch actually violated. He notes that Apple's own Remote app uses the exact same design pattern of showing a computer icon, creating a double standard where Apple can do what third-party developers cannot. While acknowledging Apple's right to reject any app, he concludes that the arbitrary enforcement undermines the platform's appeal to developers.
key_points: Airfoil Speakers Touch never contained Apple-owned images — it displayed icons sent from a connected Mac via public macOS APIs, making LaMarche's core factual claim wrong

A close reading of the iPhone SDK Agreement reveals no clause that actually prohibits what Airfoil Speakers Touch was doing; the language covers violation, misappropriation, and infringement — not mere use
Apple's own Remote app uses the identical design pattern of showing an icon of the connected computer, creating a clear double standard for third-party developers
The rejection notice language about Apple-owned graphic symbols wasn't in the SDK agreement itself — developers could only encounter it by submitting an app and getting rejected
Apple's right to reject apps for any reason is the only rule that actually matters, but exercising it arbitrarily validates Rogue Amoeba's conclusion that the platform isn't worth developing for
money_quotes: Point 1 is simply wrong; the Airfoil Speakers Touch iPhone app does not contain any of these images.
I've just re-read the entire iPhone SDK Agreement (again), and I find no clause that prohibits what Airfoil Speakers Touch was doing.
The only way Rogue Amoeba got this language was by designing, building, and submitting the application that did it.
In UI design, just as in cinema, it is almost always better to show rather than tell.
The bottom line is that they can reject apps for whatever reasons they want — that's the rule that matters here.
But was Rogue Amoeba foolish for designing their application this way? No. There's nothing in the SDK agreement that they've violated.

If the de facto rule is 'Apple can show a representation of the computer its iPhone apps connect to, but third-party developers can't', that doesn't exactly refute Rogue Amoeba's conclusion that developing for this platform just isn't worth it.
key_insight: Apple's App Store rejection of Airfoil Speakers Touch was not grounded in any actual SDK agreement violation, and the double standard with Apple's own Remote app reveals arbitrary enforcement that drives developers away from the platform.
